info.title: MeAgain Survey
info.subtitle:
Understanding the Impact of Medically
Induced Menopause from Breast Cancer Treatment for Women in Ireland

paragraphs.text: MeAgain is an early-stage start-up based in Co. Dublin, focused on understanding the impact of medically induced menopause following breast cancer treatment and developing practical support for women affected. All responses are anonymous and will be used to inform service and product design only. All questions are optional. Please do not include personal identifying information in open-text answers. This is an exploratory study and does not provide medical advice.

paragraphs.html: Your responses will directly inform the development of support programmes for women managing early-onset menopause following breast cancer treatment. If you are interested in learning more or exploring collaboration, please connect with Ana Dinho on LinkedIn or email: hello@mymeagain.ie

questions.question: 1. How old were you when menopause symptoms first began following cancer treatment?
2. Which treatments did you receive?
3. Before your diagnosis, had you ever heard of treatment-induced menopause?
options: I had heard of it but did not know the details
No — I had never heard of it

questions.question: 4. Before treatment started, were you clearly informed it could trigger early menopause and symptoms such as sleep disruption, anxiety, and hot flushes?
5. Did you feel adequately prepared for the impact of menopause symptoms before treatment started?
6. Roughly how quickly did menopause symptoms appear after treatment started?
7. When did you first realise something was not quite right? Describe briefly in your own words.
8. What felt most frightening, destabilising, or unexpected about the menopause symptoms you experienced?
options: Not understanding what was happening to my body
Anxiety / panic / loss of emotional control
Feeling like I was "not myself" anymore
Fear that this was the "new me" permanently

questions.question: 9. At what stage did you feel most unsupported in relation to menopause symptoms?
options: During hormone-blocking medication (Tamoxifen, Letrozole, Anastrozole, Exemestane)

pages.heading: SECTION C — Severity of Early-Onset Menopause Symptoms Following Cancer Treatment

questions.question: 10. Emotional & Psychological symptoms — rate each 0 (No impact) to 3 (Extremely severe / life-disrupting)
11. Cognitive symptoms — rate each 0 (No impact) to 3 (Extremely severe / life-disrupting)
12. Physical symptoms — rate each 0 (No impact) to 3 (Extremely severe / life-disrupting)
13. Any other symptoms not listed above?
14. How significantly did menopause symptoms affect the following areas of your life? (0 = No impact at all | 3 = Extremely significant impact)
15. Have menopause symptoms from your cancer treatment affected your employment?
16. Did symptoms result in reduced income?
17. When you were advised to take hormone-blocking medication (such as Tamoxifen, Letrozole, Anastrozole, Exemestane or other)
options: I decided not to start treatment
I was not offered hormone-blocking treatment

questions.question: 18. If you decided not to start, please share why (optional)
19. Are you currently taking hormone-blocking therapy?
20. Have you ever stopped hormone-blocking medication due to side effects?
21. If you stopped — approximately how long had you been taking the medication before stopping?
22. Did you consider or request HRT to help manage menopause symptoms?
23. Did you seek support specifically for treatment-induced menopause symptoms?
24. Where did you seek support?
25. During consultations about menopause symptoms related to your cancer treatment, did you feel your concerns were taken seriously?
26. Overall, how effective was the support you accessed? (Effective = helped reduce symptom severity or improved daily functioning.)
27a. If you selected "Effective", which type of support did you find effective?
options: Hospital team (e.g. oncologist, breast care nurse)

questions.question: 27b. If you selected "Effective" what specifically made this support effective for you?
options: It was personalised to my needs
The provider had expertise in treatment-induced menopause symptoms
It was offered at the right time (e.g. before or early in treatment)
Clear explanation of what to expect
Practical tools I could apply day-to-day, such as nutrition, exercise, and supplements
Easy access when I needed help
Helped me feel more in control
Improved my ability to function (work, daily life)
Educational programme to help me understand my symptoms and what to expect

questions.question: 28. If you did not access support — what factors influenced that decision?
options: I was not aware support existed
I did not know who to ask
I could not afford private support

questions.question: 29. Are you currently spending money out-of-pocket to manage menopause symptoms?
30. Approximately how much do you spend per month on private GP or private menopause clinic visits related to menopause symptoms?
31. Approximately how much do you spend per month on supplements to help manage treatment-induced menopause symptoms?

paragraphs.text: This section explores your preferences for an ideal support pathway to help manage early-onset menopause symptoms following cancer treatment. It focuses on key elements such as preparation, access to services, type of support, and ongoing management.

questions.question: 32. Thinking back to before you started your breast cancer treatment, what support do you wish had been available to help you prepare for menopause symptoms? (Select all that apply)
options: Online preparation programme (self-paced, immediate access, helping you understand what to expect before treatment starts)
One-to-one with a menopause specialist (integrated within the public cancer care pathway)
A leaflet provided by the hospital team (e.g. oncologist or breast care nurse) explaining what to expect and how menopause symptoms may affect me
A support phone line provided by the HSE to speak with a counsellor specialising in breast cancer and treatment-induced menopause
A support group (online or in-person) with women who have gone through a similar experience

questions.question: 33. Thinking back to after you started your breast cancer treatment, what support do you wish had been available to help you with your treatment induced menopause symptoms? (Select all that apply)
options: One-to-one with a menopause specialist (integrated within the public cancer care pathway)
Personalised online programme (e.g. 8 weeks) designed to help you manage menopause symptoms, feel more in control, and improve day-to-day functioning
Personalised online programme (e.g. 8 weeks) with optional nurse support (includes ~2 hours of remote nurse guidance)
A support group (online or in-person) with women who have gone through a similar experience
A support phone line provided by the HSE to speak with a counsellor specialising in breast cancer and treatment-induced menopause

questions.question: 34. If your employer offered a personalised 8-week online programme, including up to 2 hours of nurse support, to help manage menopause symptoms caused by breast cancer treatment, would you use it as part of your wellbeing benefits?

35. If your private health insurance covered a personalised 8-week online programme, including up to 2 hours of nurse support, to support menopause caused by breast cancer treatment, would you use it?

36. If a personalised 8-week online programme, including up to 2 hours of nurse support, to help manage menopause caused by breast cancer treatment were offered as part of your hospital cancer care pathway, would you use it?
36a. If "No", please specify the reason (optional)

pages.heading: SECTION I — Funding & Willingness to Pay

questions.question: 37. For each programme, who should be responsible for funding access?
rows: Personalised online programme (e.g. 8 weeks), self-paced, without nurse support
Personalised (e.g. 8-week) online programme with up to 2 hours of nurse support

questions.question: 38. If only available privately, how likely would you be to purchase one of these programmes?

39. If paying privately, what price range would you consider acceptable for an online preparation programme (self-paced) designed to help you understand and prepare for early-onset menopause resulting from cancer treatment?

40. If paying privately, what price range would you consider acceptable for a personalised online programme (e.g. 8 weeks, self-guided) designed to help you manage menopause symptoms resulting from breast cancer treatment, feel more in control, and improve your day-to-day functioning?

41. If paying privately, what price range would you consider acceptable for a personalised online programme (e.g. 8 weeks, including up to 2 hours of nurse support) designed to help you manage menopause symptoms resulting from breast cancer treatment, feel more in control, and improve your day-to-day functioning?
42. Are you currently using, or have you previously used, any app, digital tool or programme to manage menopause symptoms?

43. If comfortable, please: 1) name the tool or programme you used, 2) indicate whether it was designed for cancer-related menopause, 3) rate how helpful it was (0–3). (0 = Not helpful at all, 3 = Extremely helpful)

paragraphs.html:
Your responses will directly inform the development of support programmes for women managing early-onset menopause following breast cancer treatment.

If you are interested in learning more or exploring collaboration, please connect with Ana Dinho | LinkedIn.
